---
layout: post
title: "Typhoid Fever"
date: 2014-11-08 13:18:00 +0000
published: true
---

I continue to marvel at people that die in their 20's.&nbsp; It's not normal.&nbsp; Unfortunately, the more people I research, the more people I find passing away way too early.&nbsp; 

As was the case with William A Baty.

The only thing I knew about William for the longest time was that he was born in 1888 and died in 1914

DOB:&nbsp; 3 May 1888

DOD:&nbsp; 12 Dec 1914

In comes Newspapers.com - again they provided the critical information we've been missing!

<div class="post-image"><a target="_blank" href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=6935072490671199898" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img alt="" height="170" src="data:image/png;base64," style="cursor: move;" width="320" /></a></div>

<div class="" style="clear: both; text-align: center;">

<div class="post-image"><a target="_blank" href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=6935072490671199898" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"></a></div>

<a href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=6935072490671199898" imageanchor="1" style="clear: left; float: left; margin-bottom: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"></a><a href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=6935072490671199898" imageanchor="1" style="clear: left; float: left; margin-bottom: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"></a>
</div>

<a href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=6935072490671199898" imageanchor="1" style="clear: right; float: right; margin-bottom: 1em; margin-left: 1em;"></a>

Not only do we find William died of Typhoid Fever, but we find out that he passed away in Independence, KS. 

I'm also reading that Typhoid Fever isn't particularly pleasant.  According to the CDC

<blockquote>
Typhoid fever is a life-threatening illness caused by the bacterium Salmonella Typhi. In the United States, it is estimated that approximately 5,700 cases occur annually.  Most cases (up to 75%) are acquired while traveling internationally. Typhoid fever is still common in the developing world, where it affects about 21.5 million persons each year.

Salmonella Typhi lives only in humans. 

You can get typhoid fever if you eat food or drink beverages that have been handled by a person who is shedding Salmonella Typhi or if sewage contaminated with Salmonella Typhi bacteria gets into the water you use for drinking or washing food. Therefore, typhoid fever is more common in areas of the world where handwashing is less frequent and water is likely to be contaminated with sewage.</blockquote>

Which poses the next question, "What was William doing that he caught Typhoid Fever?"

<div style="text-align: center;">
~ Tom ~

<span style="font-family: &quot;book antiqua&quot;; font-style: italic;">CADECBAB</span>
